perturbation.original_text: Client will notify Developer in writing of any failure of a Milestone Deliverable to comply with the Specifications, or of any other objections, corrections, changes or amendments required (a Change Request), within ten (10) days of such Milestone's Acceptance Test Date.
perturbation.changed_text: Client will notify Developer in writing of any failure of a Milestone Deliverable to comply with the Specifications, or of any other objections, corrections, changes or amendments required (a Change Request), within three (3) days of such Milestone's Acceptance Test Date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time for change requests to 3 days may be too short under Washington state law regarding reasonable time to inspect goods or services.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 62A.2-602 states that acceptance of goods occurs when the buyer has had a reasonable opportunity to inspect them, and signifies that the goods are conforming or that he will take or retain them in spite of their nonconformity. Three days might not be considered a reasonable time, especially for complex software deliverables, thus potentially contradicting state law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 62A.2-602: Manner and effect of rightful rejection. Print RCWs > Title 62A > Chapter 62A.2 > Section 62A.2-602 62A.2-601 << 62A.2-602 >> 62A.2-603 PDF RCW 62A.2-602 Manner and effect of rightful rejection. (1) Rejection of goods must be within a reasonable time after their delivery or tender. It is ineffective unless the buyer seasonably notifies the seller. (2) Subject to the provisions of the two following sections on rejected goods (RCW 62A.2-603 and RCW 62A.2-604 ): (a) After rejection, any exercise of ownership by the buyer with respect to any commercial unit is wrongful as against the seller; and (b) If the buyer has before rejection taken physical possession of goods in which he or she does not have a security interest under the provisions of this Article (RCW 62A.2-711 (3)), he or she is under a duty after rejection to hold them with reasonable care at the seller's disposition for a time sufficient to permit the seller to remove them; but (c) The buyer has no further obligations with regard to goods rightfully rejected. (3) The seller's rights with respect to goods wrongfully rejected are governed by the provisions of this Article on seller's remedies in general (RCW 62A.2-703 ). [ 2013 c 23 s 148 ; 1965 ex.s. c 157 s 2-602. Cf. former RCW sections: (i) RCW 63.04.090 ; 1925 ex.s. c 142 s 8 ; RRS s 5836-8. (ii) RCW 63.04.510 ; 1925 ex.s. c 142 s 50 ; RRS s 5836-50.]
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time frame for change requests from 10 days to 3 days. The Law Explanation, supported by RCW 62A.2-602 from Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that a buyer must have a 'reasonable opportunity to inspect' goods. Reducing the time to 3 days could be deemed unreasonable, especially for complex deliverables, and thus contradicts the state law requirement for a reasonable inspection period, as it may not allow sufficient time for inspection and notification.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be immediately terminated by notice of the terminating Party upon: (a) the other Party's material breach of this Agreement and fails to cure such default within ten (10) calendar days after receipt of a notice of default from the terminating Party;
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be immediately terminated by notice of the terminating Party upon: (a) the other Party's material breach of this Agreement and fails to cure such default within two (2) calendar days after receipt of a notice of default from the terminating Party;
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to two days may be considered unreasonable in Washington state, particularly for complex breaches of contract, potentially conflicting with legal principles of fairness and reasonable opportunity to cure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles of Contracts - Cure Period
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles of Contracts - Cure Period
perturbation.law_explanation: While Washington law doesn't specify a mandatory cure period for all contract breaches, common law requires that the non-breaching party provides the breaching party with a reasonable opportunity to cure the breach. Two days is likely to be deemed unreasonable for most material breaches, especially in a complex software development agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: cure | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you cure Cure means to eliminate or correct a violation or defect . Some common uses of the term “cure” in a legal sense include: Cases such as this one from California , explain that to “cure” a default , as the term is used in the Bankruptcy Code , means that “the debtor must take care of the event triggering the default and return the parties to pre-default conditions.” In the context of contract law, the term “cure” means to correct or remove a defect that would be considered a breach by the curing party. For example, a landlord may give the tenant a set amount of time to correct, or cure, a lease violation or face an eviction lawsuit. As explained in this case from Texas , the term “cure” as used in the context of maritime law , means “the payment of necessary medical services for a seaman who is injured or becomes ill while in the service of a ship.” [Last reviewed in July of 2021 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE PROPERTY landlord & tenant contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the cure period from ten to two days, which, according to the perturbation and law explanations, is likely to be deemed unreasonable for most material breaches, potentially conflicting with legal principles of fairness and reasonable opportunity to cure. The scraped snippet defines "cure" as eliminating or correcting a violation or defect, returning parties to pre-default conditions. By shortening the cure period to two days, the changed text arguably makes it unreasonably difficult, if not impossible, for the breaching party to actually cure the default, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the reasonable opportunity to cure implied in contract law.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time during the term of this Agreement for any reason upon two weeks written notice to the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time during the term of this Agreement for any reason upon one (1) day written notice to the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only one day's notice for termination may be seen as insufficient and unconscionable, potentially conflicting with Washington state's implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington State's Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: E.g., Badgett v. Security State Bank, 116 Wn.2d 563 (Wash. 1991)
perturbation.law_explanation: Washington law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in every contract.  Terminating a significant agreement like this with only one day's notice could be seen as violating that duty, particularly if it causes undue hardship or prevents the other party from taking reasonable steps to mitigate damages. The reasonableness of notice depends on the specific facts and circumstances, but one day is unlikely to be considered sufficient.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 702. Testimony by Expert Witnesses | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 702. Testimony by Expert Witnesses A witness who is qualified as an expert by knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education may testify in the form of an opinion or otherwise if the proponent demonstrates to the court that it is more likely than not that: (a) the expert’s scientific, technical, or other specialized knowledge will help the trier of fact to understand the evidence or to determine a fact in issue; (b) the testimony is based on sufficient facts or data; (c) the testimony is the product of reliable principles and methods; and (d) the expert's opinion reflects a reliable application of the principles and methods to the facts of the case. Notes (Pub. L. 93–595, §1, Jan. 2, 1975, 88 Stat. 1937; Apr. 17, 2000, eff. Dec. 1, 2000; Apr. 26, 2011, eff. Dec. 1, 2011.) Notes of Advisory Committee on Proposed Rules An intelligent evaluation of facts is often difficult or impossible without the application of some scientific, technical, or other specialized knowledge. The most common source of this knowledge is the expert witness, although there are other techniques for supplying it. Most of the literature assumes that experts testify only in the form of opinions. The assumption is logically unfounded. The rule accordingly recognizes that an expert on the stand may give a dissertation or exposition of scientific or other principles relevant to the case, leaving the trier of fact to apply them to the facts. Since much of the criticism of expert testimony has centered upon the hypothetical question, it seems wise to recognize that opinions are not indispensable and to encourage the use of expert testimony in non-opinion form when counsel believes the trier can itself draw the requisite inference. The use of opinions is not abolished by the rule, however. It will continue to be permissible for the experts to take the further step of suggesting the inference which should be drawn from applying the specialized knowledge to the facts. See Rules 703 to 705. Whether the situation is a proper one for the use of expert testimony is to be determined on the basis of assisting the trier. “There is no more certain test for determining when experts may be used than the common sense inquiry whether the untrained layman would be qualified to determine intelligently and to the best possible degree the particular issue without enlightenment from those having a specialized understanding of the subject involved in the dispute.” Ladd, Expert Testimony, 5 Vand.L.Rev. 414, 418 (1952). When opinions are excluded, it is because they are unhelpful and therefore superfluous and a waste of time. 7 Wigmore §1918. The rule is broadly phrased. The fields of knowledge which
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided two weeks' written notice for termination, while the changed contract reduced this to one day. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that such a short notice period could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Washington law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses expert witness testimony and is irrelevant, the core issue lies in whether one day's notice is reasonable, which the explanations argue is unlikely, thereby contradicting the implied legal obligation to act in good faith.
